sentences.text: My name is Micheline Marmor.
M-A-R-M-O-R.
And the first, too?
My first name is M-I-C-H-E-L-I-N-E.
Micheline Kaplan, K-A-P-L-A-N.
Beaumont.
B-E-A-U-M-O-N-T.
Same, Micheline.
August, 20, '23.
Paris, France.
Now, I'm 73.
My-- my father is Charles Kaplan, same spelling.
Charles.
Yeah, yes.
Estelle Kaplan.
Well, before the war?
OK, well, my brother is-- well, you want the name before the war?
Sammy, [FRENCH].
OK, [FRENCH].
Kaplan.
And my sister, Ellen-- Ellen Kaplan.
Yeah, we lived in a very nice part of Paris, which is [FRENCH], [?
Cannes ?] [FRENCH], which is, if you know Paris, it's-- a street between-- it's between actually [?
Opera ?] and Madeleine.
L'Opera ?] and L'Madeleine.
Well, it's known, you know, known monument.
And we were born there.
My parents lived-- as long as they were in France, they live all their life in this apartment.
And-- and we have to move during the war, during the German occupation.
Well, we have two.
The third floor was the business.
My parents designed clothes-- high class, haute couture, we'll say-- clothes.
And this was in the third floor.
And then we live in the fifth floor.
We have, you know, the-- the bedroom and the kitchen and everything.
We-- we lived there.
Oh, yes.
Well--
Well, I share my room with my brother and my sister.
Well--
To me, it was very large.
But when I went back to France and look at it, it didn't look that large.
Like the street-- I thought the street was a big street.
And when I went back, it didn't look so big.
I thought I closed it.
Well, my-- my mother and my father and-- and the three of us.
Well, when I went to school, you know, I had my school friend.
And then after I went to study art, you know, in-- in the Beaux-Arts, which is like Cooper Union here-- the best school in France.
Then I have friend, yeah, I have friend.
Not-- not that many, no.
It's not like here, that you meet in each other house.
Everybody live privately.
Yeah, you always go to school during the day.
Actually, in France, you went to school-- I think you even went on Saturday.
But you-- you are off on Thursday.
Hm, that's a long time ago, you know?
Well, I guess I'm home.
And then I must have been reading or drawing or fighting with my brother and sister.
No, I really don't.

That's-- I remember when I-- when I study-- well, that was-- when I study painting in the private-- when we say private teacher, there was a-- the-- the woman who study with me is-- was Chinese, or, well, she may have been Japanese.
But she said she was Chinese.
[LAUGHS] And I remember [INAUDIBLE].
And [INAUDIBLE]-- let me see.
Yeah, that's all I remember.
Well, first I study, like I say, in the-- a private teacher.
And then after, I study in the Ecole de Beaux-Arts.
Yeah.
The feeling?
Well-- well--
Well, I-- I don't remember that much, that period of my life.
Oh, yes, you see, because in France-- we had the phone.
But very few people had the phone.
So then people will walk in without, you know, without calling.
You don't do this here anymore.
So there was always people coming and going.
Yes, my parents had.
But they were-- they were very busy with their business.
They didn't have that much time for free.
They have, you know, they have that business, design clothes, which was very interesting because you have a lot of people working for them.
They have the seamstress and the-- the tailor.
And they have the model-- you know the model-- the people who wear clothes?
And-- and they all-- the people who wear clothes have to speak English because a lot of our customer were American.
And that was very, very interesting.
No, no. the third--
No, the third floor.
Yeah, there was the-- everything was dedicated to the business.
Oh, yes.
People would come and-- oh yes, people would come and work.
And we have a lot of sewing machine.
And it was a, you know, it-- [? naturally, ?] you call this?
Well, where they work-- you know, all the machinery-- and things--
--like this, yeah.
And what was--
Yeah.
What was interesting that every customer have there dummy.
Some people were-- some of the customer-- they always come back.
So some were kind of fat and everything.
So it was built the way exactly the customer was built.
And then my mother would say, give me Mrs.
So-and-so, [LAUGHS] and I will bring the, you know, the dummy?
Then they could cut the dress on it.
Not-- not very much.
Oh, all together, about 60.
And we had the-- we had a man with-- actually, it was [? an Arab, ?] with the name Charles Kaplan.
And what I didn't like, he used to walk me to school.
And I didn't like this.
I don't know.
Just didn't like it.
With a uniform-- yeah, I didn't like this.
Well, my parent were believe in God, but they were not religious.
The only time we went is for Yom Kippur.
You know, you play safe.
On Yom Kippur.
Maybe another holiday.
But they were very working all the time.
They loved their work.
And they were working all the time.
So I hardly knew-- I knew I was Jewish.
But I hardly-- you know, I was not that aware because in France, you never talk about religion.
It's not polite.
You don't ask a person, what's your religion?
In this country, religion is very important.
You know, everybody has to belong to-- but not in France.
We have--
Yeah, we have a big family.
My mother has three sister.
Let's see-- one, two, three-- three sister and a brother.
In Paris.
Oh, yes, oh, yes, during the early day we have.
And then my-- my grandfather and my grandmother.
Yeah, during the early day, it was a big, big family.
That's what I miss here, you know, because I don't have a big family here.
Oh, for, like Passover, yeah, or if somebody got married, so we all meet.
It was a lot, a lot of family because they all have children.
Yeah.
Well, for special occasion, not-- I told you, my parents were very busy [LAUGHS] working.
Yes.
My sister more because she was older.
Oh, yes, we were very close.
I don't remember.
I know we stay home.
It's not like in this country, we go from one friend to another.
More, everybody stay in their one family.
I play with my brother and my sister is all.
Oh, yes, I have a special girlfriend, which we talk on the phone a lot.
And, yeah, I forget I meet her.
But that was--
Yes, she'd have been Jewish, yes.
And you know, it's very way.
And I don't remember that much about it.
No, we have-- [LAUGHS] my parents-- actually, we are wealthy because of the business.
So we have a cordon bleu cooking because, actually, all the model-- you call, like, the model-- people who put the-- show the clothes?-- they were eating at noon.
So the cook was cooking-- was cooking for all these people.
And--
No, just during the week.
But I remember is we have to deliver some clothes.
And if it was not ready, they would work most of the night-- everybody.
Sometime.
[LAUGHS] But it was-- it was really an interesting life, anyway.
You know, there's a lot of going-- coming and going.
Yeah, and a lot of them come from the United State and all over the world, they came.
They-- they had a good reputation as the, you know?
Yeah.
All type.
My mother was doing the dress and the evening dress.
And my father was doing the suit with, you know, there was a tailor there, too.
Yeah.
Everything-- everything is custom-made just for one person.
They got to choose the design and then the material.
And this is the way it work.
Well, it must have been.
But I-- you know, [LAUGHS] I don't remember.
But I know it-- it must have been expensive, yes.
Well--
Well, yeah, some teacher were anti-Semite.
Well, they were saying-- I don't remember.
But I know it was not comfortable because they-- they know we were Jewish.
And they must have done some remark.
I cannot say what, but I felt it.
My sister was the oldest one.
And then my brother.
My sister is three years older.
And my brother is four years younger.
Well, yeah, I think so.
Yeah, they must have had friend.
But--
Well, I thought it was big till I came back 25 years later.
It was a very nice street.
And there was-- right in that street, there is a store called [FRENCH], which is very known, even in this country, where they serve very good food-- a special kind of food.
And at the end, there was a Cinema Le Madeleine, a movie theater called Le Madeleine.
And at the very other end of the street was a department store called [FRENCH]-- the very end.
And not too far was [?
Le ?] Gare Saint-Lazare-- Saint-Lazare Station.
No, not the ground floor.
We live in the third floor.
Yeah, yes.
And the business-- the business was in the fifth floor-- the whole-- the whole-- you know, the whole fifth floor.
It was a big house-- I mean, a big apartment house.
And then the fifth floor was where we live-- eat and sleep.
And then my parents got some customer who didn't want to walk-- especially American customer-- they didn't want to walk three floor.
So my parents built the-- an elevator.
It's still there when I-- [LAUGHS] that was my parents' doing.
They built an elevator.
Yes.
Yeah, the whole-- the whole house.
Well, the-- let's see-- third-- the second floor was somebody doing millinery-- doing hat.
And the four-- the fourth floor some-- it was somebody doing-- how do you call corset, brassier, and all kind of thing like this.
Yeah.
And then we have what you call maid room.
Maybe we had two maid room at the very top, you know.
Yeah, the sixth floor.
I remember because it became important during the war, this-- this-- actually, when I stop-- well, we had maid.
But not-- later on, we didn't.
So I ask my mother to let me use one room, that I make like a little studio to-- to paint and draw.
How old were you then when you-- when you did that?
Well, 16, about, 17.
This part is not very clear, you know, because I didn't think about it.
You didn't tell me you were going to ask me, [LAUGHS] so I didn't think about it.
Well, what I remember is every room has a fireplace, which is nice.
And then we have-- oh, and the kitchen was-- well, in the-- yeah, across the-- you have the stair, and across were all the-- the material was-- you know, wool and silk.
We had everything there.
Later on, we use is as a kitchen.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, yes.
For-- for-- for France, it was a big-- big place.
I know because during the war, you have to-- to light the, you know, the fire.
Bring-- how do you call it?
You know-- [FRENCH], and light the fire.
Yeah, every room had a fireplace.
Well, I guess most place must have been like this.
I always like fireplaces.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
Well-- well, you know, the war started in '39, I think.
The declaration of war with France started in 1939.
But nothing happened for a whole year.
So we were living normally.
And there was no change in your life at all--
No. --during that period?
No, we had no change because we-- we never dreamed that the German will, you know, will-- will come to France.
So I think in 19-- I forget when the German came to Paris.
I have the date.
Well, I-- I have the-- anyway, when the German came to Paris, everything was still normal for almost a year.
But they started-- they started very, very slowly.
Yeah.
Oh, yes, when they came to-- to Paris, we-- and actually, they were very proper, you know, they were very polite.
They will give you-- they will give you your seat in the subway.
And no problem in the beginning.
They-- they were very, very nice, very proper-- in the beginning.
[FRENCH] it's called.
I really don't know where they lived.
But I saw them.
Yeah, they were all over Paris.
And- what was I doing then?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No.
I mean, they just-- they-- they were all over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So can I look at the date at something?
Not good, eh?
Yeah.
Oh, OK, so--
--the beginning was-- oh, first was the propaganda.
After a while, they-- they started what you call propaganda.
So they started to say horrible thing against the Jew.
Like they will say, the Jew are dirty.
They have greasy hair.
Greasy hair.
Greasy hair.
Hook nose.
And all the problem in-- in France is-- everywhere in the world-- is because of the Jew.
So in the beginning, people were, you know, they were-- didn't take it seriously.
It was like a joke.
On the radio.
Well, yeah, there was only the French radio there.
So you listen.
And-- but little by lit, people start to believe it.
You know, when you repeat, repeat.
And some people were glad to believe it, too.
Well, it was in French.
I don't know who say it.
I guess the-- I don't if it-- it must have been the-- the collaborator, I guess.
Is that the same one?
Yeah, the collaborator.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, well, it was a very, very slow process.
They started by-- let's see when they started by.
Yeah, all of a sudden, they say, every-- every Jew has to go to the police station and just register-- nothing else.
And, you know, you always obey the law in France, you-- so we all went.
If we have known-- [LAUGHS].
We all went and put our name.
Then, after this, let's see, the next-- the next thing was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yeah.
Well, the government.
Yeah.
No, the French, not the German.
The-- the-- the-- the-- the French followed the-- the French law when they ask.
Well, I-- I really don't remember.
You know, I was about-- I was 16, [? nigh. ?] So then the next thing was-- let me see.
If I could do it [? connect-- ?] after this, it was-- let me see-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, well, you have to give up your-- your radio.
You know, there was no television.
You have to give up your radio.
First, we kept it anyway.
OK.
Let me see, just because I put it chronologically.
Oh, yeah, something else.
I knew police station, [? about. ?] OK, well, there's something else happened in my-- see, it's good if I can look at the--
Yeah.
Something else happened.
All of a sudden, they put a-- an Aryan administrator.
My parents work all their life to-- to build a beautiful business.
And then they put, you know, Aryan in the same name, right?
Aryan-- non-Jewish-- administrator.
And my parents were not in charge anymore.
That woman was-- who came, and then direct everything.
Was it-- was that, a woman, did you say?
Yeah.
[FRENCH]
Yeah, yes.
And then you have to write in front of the door, Jewish business in German-- I don't know it, forget-- that everybody knows that it was.
So that was really disturbing.
You know, the place didn't belong to you anymore.
OK, let me tell you.
I have to read.
In April '41-- April '41.
The-- when they put somebody.
OK?
And then after, let's see.
[FRENCH] The new law came in June, where you have to register in the police station.
All this was '41.
And then August 13-- you see how slowly it go?
August 13 was confiscation of all radio.
OK, the two last-- the two last laws was the-- the most difficult.
Oh, OK.
Good.
How do you--
